one_line_takeaway: A 2025 survey that taxonomizes LLM unlearning into four method families and a classical-vs-adversarial evaluation split, arguing that most methods trade forgetting against utility and that adversarial probes (relearning, quantization, MIA, jailbreak) often show forgetting is mere suppression, not removal.
key_finding: There is a persistent trade-off between forgetting quality and utility preservation, and no method dominates. Per the survey's Table 2 qualitative rating: Gradient Ascent gives High robust forgetting but Low utility (degrades the model); Preference Optimization and Reinforcement Learning give High utility but only Medium forgetting; Locate-then-Unlearn is the only family rated High on BOTH utility preservation and robust forgetting, at the cost of Medium efficiency (expensive attribution); auxiliary-model methods (contrastive decoding, knowledge distillation) score Low efficiency; input/output-based (guardrail) methods are High utility and High efficiency but Low robust forgetting. Crucially, adversarial evaluation (relearning, quantization, MIA, jailbreak) frequently reverses apparent unlearning, so forgetting is often superficial suppression.
core_contribution: A survey (not an empirical study) of machine unlearning for LLMs. Its own contributions are: (1) a formalization of the LLM-unlearning optimization objective and two operating paradigms (fine-tuning-then-unlearning vs. direct-unlearning); (2) a four-way method taxonomy (direct fine-tuning, localized parameter modification, leveraging auxiliary models, input/output-based unlearning); (3) a structured review of evaluation measures split into classical vs. adversarial, plus a catalog of unimodal and multimodal benchmarks; and (4) a comparison (Table 1) positioning itself against four prior surveys, claiming broader coverage (10 methodology families and 7 adversarial-evaluation methods vs. 3-6 methods and 0-5 adversarial methods in prior surveys). Note: the technical method descriptions (GA, DPO/NPO, RMU, DEPN, WAGLE, ULD, RKLD, task vectors, Ethos, in-context unlearning, etc.) are summaries of CITED work, not the survey's own algorithms.
threat_model: The paper's security-relevant framing is that 'unlearning' may only superficially suppress rather than truly remove undesirable knowledge (privacy/PII, copyrighted text, hazardous bio/cyber/chemical knowledge, toxic or jailbreak-enabling content). The adversary's goal is to recover ostensibly forgotten information. The survey organizes the recovery threat surface into: input-based attacks (paraphrasing/translation, jailbreak prompts including role-play and optimized prefixes), logit-based probes (KS-Test, membership-inference attacks), and model-intervention attacks (benign relearning/fine-tuning, low-bit quantization e.g. 4-bit, neuron pruning, and activation/refusal-direction steering). A cited finding it highlights: many unlearning methods fail under MIA and under quantization, indicating suppression rather than removal.
limitations: Self-declared: no original experiments, so the survey provides no empirical validation of the concepts it organizes; and possible omissions given the field's fast pace. Additional reviewer-noted gaps: Table 2's utility/forgetting/efficiency ratings are coarse qualitative labels (High/Medium/Low) with no underlying numbers or unified experimental protocol; and much of the technical detail is a restatement of cited primary work rather than new synthesis, so entity attribution matters when mining this paper.

datasets_used: WHP (Who's Harry Potter) - benchmark reviewed, not run
quantitative_results: Self-comparison (Table 1): this survey covers 10 methodology families and 7 adversarial-evaluation methods, vs. 3-6 methodology families and 0-5 adversarial methods in the four prior surveys
WHP benchmark: 2.1M tokens of original Harry Potter books + 1M synthetic tokens; evaluated on 330 HP-related questions scored by GPT-4
TOFU benchmark: 200 synthetic author profiles x 20 QA examples each, plus 100 real-world author profiles and 117 world facts (~4,000 total volume in Table 3)
WMDP benchmark: 3,668 multiple-choice questions across biosecurity, cybersecurity, and chemical security
RWKU benchmark: 200 unlearning targets (public figures), 13,131 multi-level forget probes and 11,379 neighbor probes
FIUBench benchmark: 400 synthetic faces, 20 QA pairs each (8,000 volume)
MLLMU-Bench benchmark: 500 fictitious + 153 celebrity profiles, 20,754 questions total, 3.5k paraphrased questions and 500 pose-modified images
CLEAR benchmark: 200 fictitious individuals, 3.7k visual QA + 4k textual QA pairs (7,700 volume)
DEPN uses m=20 gradient-integration steps to approximate the neuron privacy-attribution integral
NPO (Zhang et al. 2024a): shown theoretically to converge to GA as beta->0, but collapse is exponentially slower than GA
baselines_compared: Prior surveys compared in Table 1: Si et al. (2023), Xu (2024), Liu et al. (2025), Liu et al. (2024c) - the survey has no experimental baselines of its own
